# AIchitect

> AI tools are all over the place. AIchitect maps the full ecosystem so developers can pick the right stack without the noise.

AIchitect is a free, open-source, interactive tool landscape for AI developers. It catalogs 123 AI tools across 12 categories and maps their integrations, so teams can understand the ecosystem and make informed stack decisions.

GitHub: https://github.com/letsgogeeky/aichitect

## What AIchitect covers

- **123 AI tools** spanning code editors, agent frameworks, LLM providers, observability, vector databases, MCP servers, and more
- **12 tool categories**: Coding Assistants, Autonomous Agents, Agent Frameworks, Pipelines & RAG, LLM Infrastructure, Design & UI, DevOps & CI/CD, Documentation, Product & PM, MCP Servers, Prompt & Eval, Specifications
- **3 relationship types** between tools: integrates-with, often-used-together, competes-with
- **~283 mapped relationships** showing which tools integrate, pair well, or compete
- **25 curated stacks** organized as mission briefings across 5 decision clusters
- **Stack Builder**: compose a custom stack slot-by-slot and see live integration graphs with shareable URLs
- **Tool Comparison**: compare any two tools side by side — pricing, integrations, shared ecosystem connections, and direct relationships

## Pages

- / — Landing page with links to all views
- /explore — Full interactive graph of all 123 tools with category and relationship filters; supports 2D grid, 2D layers, and 3D rotation views
- /stacks — 25 mission-driven, pre-built AI stacks organized by decision cluster, each with situation brief, explicit rejections, kill conditions, and graduation paths
- /builder — Build a custom stack by picking one tool per slot; renders live connection graph; share via URL
- /compare/[tool-a]/[tool-b] — Dedicated comparison page for any two tools; full pricing, relationship, and ecosystem data

## Stacks — 5 clusters, 25 missions

Stacks are organized as mission briefings. Each stack describes the specific situation it solves, the tools chosen, what was explicitly rejected and why, and when to move on.

### Build — "Ship this week"
For solo developers and small teams who need to move fast.
- Indie Hacker / Startup Stack — 2 weeks, 1 engineer, 3 months of runway
- AI Design-to-Code Pipeline — Figma to production components without translation loss
- MCP Power User Stack — one protocol connecting all your tools
- No-Code AI Automation Stack — AI workflows without writing code
- TypeScript-Only AI Stack — full AI capability without a Python service
- Zero-Budget OSS Stack — capable AI development at $0/mo in API costs

### Automate — "AI does the work"
For teams delegating work to agents and closing autonomous loops.
- Agentic Coding Stack — task to reviewed PR without human keyboard input
- Multi-Agent DevOps Stack — triage, fix, and review agents in parallel
- Browser AI / Web Agent Stack — navigate, scrape, and fill forms with no human in the loop
- Voice AI Pipeline — speech in, intelligence out, speech back, streaming
- Data + AI Pipeline — warehouse as context source, LLM as reasoning layer

### Ship & Harden — "Make it trustworthy"
For teams who are in production and need reliability, quality, and cost control.
- LLM Production Infra Stack — provider-agnostic, swap models with a config change
- Evaluation & Quality Stack — regression testing and quality metrics for LLM features
- Spec-Driven AI Development — spec as source of truth, everything downstream stays in sync
- LLM Cost Reduction Stack — cut API spend 50–80% without degrading quality
- Multi-Modal RAG Stack — RAG over PDFs, images, tables, and charts
- Legacy App + AI Stack — add AI to an existing Rails/Django/Java app without a rewrite

### Comply & Restrict — "Nothing leaves the building"
For teams with data residency, compliance, or security requirements.
- OSS Self-Hosted AI Stack — zero data egress, everything on-premise
- EU / GDPR Regulated AI Stack — cloud AI using EU-resident providers with signed DPAs
- Edge / On-Device AI Stack — model runs on the device, no server round-trip
- AI Red-Team / Security Stack — test your own AI for prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data leakage

### Understand — "Data is the product"
For teams building knowledge systems, training custom models, or extracting structured data at scale.
- Enterprise RAG Stack — production RAG with routing, eval, and observability
- Fine-Tuning Pipeline — when prompting hits its ceiling, you train
- Document Intelligence Stack — structured extraction from contracts, invoices, and records at volume
- Research & Synthesis Stack — AI that reads across sources and produces structured reports

## URL patterns

- /stacks?cluster=build — stacks in the Build cluster
- /stacks?cluster=automate — stacks in the Automate cluster
- /stacks?cluster=ship — stacks in the Ship & Harden cluster
- /stacks?cluster=comply — stacks in the Comply & Restrict cluster
- /stacks?cluster=understand — stacks in the Understand cluster
- /stacks?stack=indie-hacker — specific stack selected
- /explore?compare=cursor,github-copilot — compare two tools in the interactive graph
- /compare/cursor/github-copilot — dedicated SEO page comparing two specific tools
- /builder?s=cursor,langgraph,openai-api — pre-loaded stack in the builder

## Tool comparison: key pairs

- Cursor vs GitHub Copilot: /compare/cursor/github-copilot
- LangChain vs LlamaIndex: /compare/langchain/llamaindex
- OpenAI API vs Anthropic API: /compare/openai-api/anthropic-api
- CrewAI vs AutoGen: /compare/crewai/autogen
- Devin vs OpenHands: /compare/devin/openhands
- LiteLLM vs OpenRouter: /compare/litellm/openrouter
- Langfuse vs LangSmith: /compare/langfuse/langsmith
- Qdrant vs pgvector: /compare/qdrant/pgvector
- Ollama vs vLLM: /compare/ollama/vllm

## Tool categories and example tools

- Coding Assistants: Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Aider, Continue, Zed
- Autonomous Agents: Devin, OpenHands, GPT Pilot, SWE-agent
- Agent Frameworks: LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Mastra, PydanticAI, Agno, smolagents
- Pipelines & RAG: LangChain, LlamaIndex, Dify, n8n, Flowise, DSPy, Haystack
- LLM Infrastructure: OpenAI API, Anthropic API, Mistral API, Together AI, Fireworks AI, Ollama, vLLM, llama.cpp, LiteLLM, OpenRouter, Replicate
- Observability: LangSmith, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, Helicone, Logfire
- Vector Databases: Qdrant, pgvector, Chroma, Weaviate, Milvus, Pinecone
- Prompt & Eval: PromptFoo, DeepEval, RAGAS, Weights & Biases, Vellum, Agenta
- MCP Servers: MCP SDK TypeScript, MCP SDK Python, GitHub MCP, Playwright MCP, E2B, Smithery, FastMCP
- Design & UI: v0, Locofy, Framer AI, Relume, Builder.io, Uizard
- DevOps & CI/CD: CodeRabbit, Sweep AI, Qodo, Graphite, Trunk, Modal, Ray
- Specifications: Swagger / OpenAPI, Speakeasy, Spec Kit, Fern, Stainless, Redocly

## Stack data shape

Each stack has:
- id, name, description, target — identity and audience
- cluster — one of: build | automate | ship | comply | understand
- mission — the specific situation and constraints the stack was assembled for
- not_in_stack — tools that were considered and explicitly rejected, with one-line reasons
- kill_conditions — the specific conditions that signal it's time to graduate to a different stack
- graduates_to — stack id of the natural next stack in the progression
- why — reasoning behind the tool choices
- tradeoffs — honest description of where this stack falls short
- complexity — beginner | intermediate | advanced
- monthly_cost — approximate cost range
- tools — ordered list of tool ids in the stack
- flow — directed edges describing how tools connect in this stack

## Tool data shape

Each tool has: id, name, category, tagline, description, type (oss|saas), pricing (free_tier, plans[]), github_stars, slot, urls (website, github)

Each relationship has: source (tool id), target (tool id), type (integrates-with|commonly-paired|competes-with)

## Brand

AIchitect's core thesis: the AI tooling space is noisy and overwhelming. Developers waste time evaluating tools that don't work well together. AIchitect solves this with a structured, visual map of the ecosystem — so you can pick your stack based on real integration data, not hype.

Tagline: "Cut the noise. Pick your AI stack."

## Open Source

AIchitect is MIT-licensed and fully open source. Contributions, issues, and new tool suggestions are welcome via GitHub.
